title: Silhouette portrait papercutting
subtitle: Cutting a profile silhouette from a projected or traced outline
excerpt: A silhouette portrait is a profile shadow cut from black paper. The outline can be traced from a projected shadow, drawn from a photograph, or drawn freehand. The cut is taken as a single unbroken outline from neck to top of head, with any internal details such as hair texture added separately with small scissors.
sourceNotes: Silhouette portraiture as a paper-cutting tradition reached its peak in 18th-century Europe; the technique of projecting a profile shadow for tracing is documented in period manuals. Augustin de Saint-Aubin's profile-cutting methods and contemporary instructional texts are pre-1928.

glossaryTerms.definition: A solid-filled profile portrait, traditionally cut from black paper. The name comes from Etienne de Silhouette, an 18th-century French minister associated with economical cut-paper portraits.

The continuous outline of a face seen from the side, running from the chin, over the lips and nose, across the brow, over the top of the head, and down the back of the skull to the neck. The profile line is the single most important element of a recognisable silhouette portrait.

content.text: portrait depends entirely on the accuracy of the

. A recognisable likeness lives in the specific shapes of the subject's nose, brow, and chin in profile; these are the features that distinguish one silhouette from another. Getting the outline right before cutting (by tracing, projecting, or careful drawing from a photograph) is more important than cutting technique. Once the outline is accurate, the cutting itself is straightforward.

items.name: Black paper or card, 120 to 160 gsm (heavier card is harder to cut cleanly around fine hair details)
Craft knife with a sharp blade
Small pointed scissors (for hair detail and concave curves)
Pencil (for tracing the outline onto the back of the black paper)
Tracing paper or light box (for transferring a reference outline)

content.text: Print or display a clear side-profile photograph at the intended final size. Tape tracing paper over it and trace the profile line carefully, including hair outline and any distinguishing features such as glasses or a beard.
Transfer the traced outline onto the back of the black paper using a light box, or by pressing hard enough with the pencil to leave a groove.

Cut the outer profile line first, working from the neck up and over the top of the head and down the back. Use the craft knife for long smooth curves; the pointed scissors for tight concave areas such as the underside of the nose or the nape of the neck.
Cut any hair texture by making small V-shaped nicks with the pointed scissors along the hair outline. Keep the nicks shallow: cutting too deep removes too much of the silhouette shape.
Mount the cut silhouette on white paper or card using a glue stick, pressing flat without wrinkling. Trim the mount to a standard frame size if matting.

attrs.heading: Common problems with silhouette cutting
attrs.intro: Problems in the profile line and cutting technique.

items.symptom: Outline has jagged edges rather than smooth curves
items.cause: Craft knife blade used for short choppy strokes rather than a single flowing cut.
items.fix: Use longer strokes, allowing the blade to follow the line rather than steering it. Replace the blade if it skips or drags.

items.symptom: Likeness is not recognisable despite accurate tracing
items.cause: Reference photograph not in true profile (even a slight turn of the head changes the profile line significantly).
items.fix: Use a strictly side-on photograph. Any visible second eye in the reference means the head is not in true profile.
